2. Zero hunger.summary: The evidence provided does not directly measure the change in SOC stock due to sustainable soil management practices. The SOC stock level in agricultural lands within the project area before and after the implementation of sustainable soil management practices is not explicitly stated. However, the percentage change in Soil Organic Carbon (SOC) stock in agricultural lands within the project area during the reporting period is mentioned, with an increase of 4.0% from 2020 to 2021 and a decrease of 8.0% from 2021 to 2022. The baseline SOC stock level is 10.5 tdm/ha, but this is not directly related to the change in SOC stock. Therefore, the evidence is treated as qualitative, and the score is 1.

Improvement through reforestation  ID 3: Soil condition: Soil organic  3.  carbon/matter content: A soil sampling  campaign has been implemented in both years: 2020 and 2021 (90 sampling points). The  results stated that soil organic matter  increased at the Finca (from 5.54% in 2020 to  5.72% in 2021). This increase is not yet  significant, but it is an indicator that soil  organic matter did probably not decline.   a.  Evidence 05  ID 4: Biodiversity: Indicator: Tree Crown  4.  cover. This analysis was not possible as we  assumed using the satellite images of Google  Earth where however the most recent updated  satellite image for the planting area is  available for the year 2015. There are no  further alternative options available on an  annual basis at alternative sites, as  globalforestwatch.org (GFW). At GFW there  are also specific biodiversity indicators  available, however, not on an annual basis.   However, as an alternative, a biodiversity  study with the University National Agraria was  implemented. In this study fauna and floral  species have been specified. The results  revealed the importance that farms like this  one can have, which are committed to the  protection and sustainable use of natural  resources. Several species have been found,  some of them are even in serious danger of  extinction. Add to this the large amount of  precious wood found, which in many cases is  the object of illegal trafficking, but this is not  the case on this farm. Undoubtedly, this  reveals that a good step has been taken  towards sustainability, using these production  systems.  a. Hence, it is concluded that the risk of change to the project  area is very limited.: The  project  is  located  on  private,  legally  purchased  land  with the purpose to produce cacao for own production.   As  long  as  the  production  of  cocoa  on  the  project  area  is  attractive the PP will have no incentive to change the land  use.  Moreover  the  project  area  is  divided  in  planting  and  protection  area  with  natural  and  second  forests.    The  mixing of the land use is increasing resistance of  thecocoa  plantation against natural disasters.   During  the  on-site  inspection,  it  was  observed  that  the  project management is sound and professional /WC//JMR/. | OK: OK

1.14 | Please provide a summary of  the monitoring information  collected during the year.:  | ID 1: Air quality. Improvement through  1.  reforestation, no measurement  ID 2: Water quality and quantity:  2.  Improvement through reforestation  ID 3: Soil condition: Soil organic  3.  carbon/matter content: A soil sampling  campaign has been implemented in different: years 2020, 2021 and 2022 (90 sampling  points). The results stated that soil organic  matter increased at the Finca (from 5.54% in  2020 to 5.72% in 2021, although in 2022 a  reduction to 5.24% was reported. This could  have been due to the following factors:  Adverse climatic conditions (very high  precipitation, temperatures, and radiation);  potential sampling uncertainties. In addition,  due to the high amount of rain recorded, a  washout of bases occurs that increases the  acidity in the soil and decreases the pH, both  with negative results.   ID 4: Biodiversity: Indicator: Tree Crown  4.  cover. This analysis was not possible as we  assumed using the satellite images of Google  Earth where however the most recent updated  satellite image for the planting area is  available for the year 2015. There are no  further alternative options available on an  annual basis at alternative sites, as  globalforestwatch.org (GFW). At GFW there  are also specific biodiversity indicators  available, however, not on an annual basis.   However, as an alternative, a biodiversity  study with the University National Agraria was  implemented. In this study fauna and floral  species have been specified. The results  revealed the importance that farms like this  one can have, which are committed to the  protection and sustainable use of natural  resources. Several species have been found,  some of them are even in serious danger of  extinction. Add to this the large amount of  precious wood found, which in many cases is  the object of illegal trafficking, but this is not  the case on this farm. Undoubtedly, this  reveals that a good step has been taken  towards sustainability, using these production  systems.    ID 5: Quality of employment: The number of  5.  person-hours training was 1164h (for 38  persons) in 2022 (English language courses,  training on quality control of cocoa beans,  national law etc.)  ID 6: Livelihood of the poor: + ID 8:  6.

1.8 |  Please list the recent activities,  events and actions related to  the project that summarize the  project’s progress during the  monitoring period this report  refers to.:  | Activities:  Fertirrigation  project:  The  project  executed  in  •  2021  is  about  a  fertigation  system  in  cocoa  plantations  under  agroforestry  systems,  this  was  established  in  order  to  achieve  better  nutrient  assimilation  and  to  be  able  to  give  better soil management. The farm has a water  reservoir  which  is  working  in  the  supply  for  pumping  water  for  fertigation.  An  electrical  network was installed up to the site where the  fertigation  system  pumps  were  installed.  For  this  reason,  according  to  the  environmental  evaluation  system  (Decree  20-2017  that  establishes  the  bases  that  govern  the  environmental  evaluation  of  projects  and  activities in the country), it was classified as a  Project  of  moderate  potential  environmental  impact, the project was subject to a study for  the  processing  of  the  permit  application  according to the established procedures.   After  planting  wild  forage  peanuts  on  about  •  146  ha  of  the  cocoa  plantation  in  2019,  this  project was continued in 2021 with further 88  ha added. The goal is that by the end of 2023  the whole cocoa plantation will be planted with  wild forage peanuts. The benefits obtained from  this project are: Weed control, labor reduction,  soil  conservation,  and  Disease  control.  Furthermore,  according  to  the  studies  carried  out,  wild  forage  peanut  provides  us  1.44  nitrogen  kg/ha  and  an  increase  of  24%  of  organic matter in the soil.  Compost application: 123.5 tons of compost  •  were applied as equal amounts at the non- planting area in the Finca (locally produced  out of cocoa pods, organic waste and Gandul  tree, which is grown for biomasse purposes))  •  Soil analysis: Every year one big soil sampling  is carried out, this is being analyzed by a  laboratory in Costa Rica for macro and  micronutrients such as phosphorus nitrogen,  potassium, zinc, boron, calcium, magnesium,  manganese and iron. We also carry out three  internal analyzes where we monitor the PH  and the extractable acidity.  Evidence 05  o    •  Water analysis. There were twelve analyzes of  drinking water, which were carried out with  the farm’s internal laboratory. The water of  the river was last analyzed in 2020.   Evidence 06  o  New permanente jobs: 35 compared to 2020.  •  2000 hours of trainings for employees: Due to  •:

2.1.1 (f)  Risk  of  change  to  the  project  area  (during  the  crediting period): 2.1.1 (g)  Risk  of  change  to  the  project  activities  (during  the crediting period) | The land purchase contracts /LPC/ was checked, it illustrated  that  the  land  ownership  is  founded  on  solid  legal  basis.  Hence,  it  is  confirmed  that  the  signed  agreement  has  a  sufficiently binding character to minimize the risk of change  to the project area (during the crediting period).   The  map  in  the  key  project  information  has  been  crosschecked.  Based  on  the  local  expertise  of  the  validation  team,  it  is  further  demonstrated  that  once  the  land  was  legally  purchased  no  one  can  change  the  land  ownership unless the PP decides to sell it.   Hence, it is concluded that the risk of change to the project  area is very limited.: The  project  is  located  on  private,  legally  purchased  land  with the purpose to produce cacao for own production.   As  long  as  the  production  of  cocoa  on  the  project  area  is  attractive the PP will have no incentive to change the land  use.  Moreover  the  project  area  is  divided  in  planting  and  protection  area  with  natural  and  second  forests.    The  mixing of the land use is increasing resistance of  thecocoa  plantation against natural disasters.   During  the  on-site  inspection,  it  was  observed  that  the  project management is sound and professional /WC//JMR/. | OK: OK

1.14 | Please provide a summary of  the monitoring information  collected during the year.:  | ID 1: Air quality. Improvement through  1.  reforestation, no measurement  ID 2: Water quality and quantity:  2.  Improvement through reforestation  ID 3: Soil condition: Soil organic  3.  carbon/matter content: A soil sampling  campaign has been implemented in different: years 2020, 2021 and 2022 (90 sampling  points). The results stated that soil organic  matter increased at the Finca (from 5.54% in  2020 to 5.72% in 2021, although in 2022 a  reduction to 5.24% was reported. This could  have been due to the following factors:  Adverse climatic conditions (very high  precipitation, temperatures, and radiation);  potential sampling uncertainties. In addition,  due to the high amount of rain recorded, a  washout of bases occurs that increases the  acidity in the soil and decreases the pH, both  with negative results.   ID 4: Biodiversity: Indicator: Tree Crown  4.  cover. This analysis was not possible as we  assumed using the satellite images of Google  Earth where however the most recent updated  satellite image for the planting area is  available for the year 2015. There are no  further alternative options available on an  annual basis at alternative sites, as  globalforestwatch.org (GFW). At GFW there  are also specific biodiversity indicators  available, however, not on an annual basis.   However, as an alternative, a biodiversity  study with the University National Agraria was  implemented. In this study fauna and floral  species have been specified. The results  revealed the importance that farms like this  one can have, which are committed to the  protection and sustainable use of natural  resources. Several species have been found,  some of them are even in serious danger of  extinction. Add to this the large amount of  precious wood found, which in many cases is  the object of illegal trafficking, but this is not  the case on this farm. Undoubtedly, this  reveals that a good step has been taken  towards sustainability, using these production  systems.    ID 5: Quality of employment: The number of  5.  person-hours training was 1164h (for 38  persons) in 2022 (English language courses,  training on quality control of cocoa beans,  national law etc.)  ID 6: Livelihood of the poor: + ID 8:  6.

2.1.1 (f)  Risk  of  change  to  the  project  area  (during  the  crediting period): 2.1.1 (j)  Land-use  history  and  current  situation  of  the  project area | The land purchase contracts /LPC/ was checked, it illustrated  that  the  land  ownership  is  founded  on  solid  legal  basis.  Hence,  it  is  confirmed  that  the  signed  agreement  has  a  sufficiently binding character to minimize the risk of change  to the project area (during the crediting period).   The  map  in  the  key  project  information  has  been  crosschecked.  Based  on  the  local  expertise  of  the  validation  team,  it  is  further  demonstrated  that  once  the  land  was  legally  purchased  no  one  can  change  the  land  ownership unless the PP decides to sell it.   Hence, it is concluded that the risk of change to the project  area is very limited.: Via checking the Map of Land use/MAP/, aerial images from  1970 and 2011/AI/ and interview with local stakeholders it is  confirmed that the land use history is correctly stated in the  document of Key Project Information.  As can be confirmed  via  onsite  inspection  the  land-use  prior  to  the  start  of  the  plantation was use for cattle farming and slash and burn of  the  area  for  land  clearing  and  subsequent  clearing.  This  has  been  substantiated  by  interview  with  a  previous  farm  manager, local people living next to the farm as well as by  provided land contracts.  Via  checking  the  project  implementation  plan/PPST/,  it  is  confirmed  that  the  current  situation  of  the  project  area  is  correct. | OK: OK

1.8 |  Please list the recent activities,  events and actions related to  the project that summarize the  project’s progress during the  monitoring period this report  refers to.:  | Activities:  Fertirrigation  project:  The  project  executed  in  •  2021  is  about  a  fertigation  system  in  cocoa  plantations  under  agroforestry  systems,  this  was  established  in  order  to  achieve  better  nutrient  assimilation  and  to  be  able  to  give  better soil management. The farm has a water  reservoir  which  is  working  in  the  supply  for  pumping  water  for  fertigation.  An  electrical  network was installed up to the site where the  fertigation  system  pumps  were  installed.  For  this  reason,  according  to  the  environmental  evaluation  system  (Decree  20-2017  that  establishes  the  bases  that  govern  the  environmental  evaluation  of  projects  and  activities in the country), it was classified as a  Project  of  moderate  potential  environmental  impact, the project was subject to a study for  the  processing  of  the  permit  application  according to the established procedures.   After  planting  wild  forage  peanuts  on  about  •  146  ha  of  the  cocoa  plantation  in  2019,  this  project was continued in 2021 with further 88  ha added. The goal is that by the end of 2023  the whole cocoa plantation will be planted with  wild forage peanuts. The benefits obtained from  this project are: Weed control, labor reduction,  soil  conservation,  and  Disease  control.  Furthermore,  according  to  the  studies  carried  out,  wild  forage  peanut  provides  us  1.44  nitrogen  kg/ha  and  an  increase  of  24%  of  organic matter in the soil.  Compost application: 123.5 tons of compost  •  were applied as equal amounts at the non- planting area in the Finca (locally produced  out of cocoa pods, organic waste and Gandul  tree, which is grown for biomasse purposes))  •  Soil analysis: Every year one big soil sampling  is carried out, this is being analyzed by a  laboratory in Costa Rica for macro and  micronutrients such as phosphorus nitrogen,  potassium, zinc, boron, calcium, magnesium,  manganese and iron. We also carry out three  internal analyzes where we monitor the PH  and the extractable acidity.  Evidence 05  o    •  Water analysis. There were twelve analyzes of  drinking water, which were carried out with  the farm’s internal laboratory. The water of  the river was last analyzed in 2020.   Evidence 06  o  New permanente jobs: 35 compared to 2020.  •  2000 hours of trainings for employees: Due to  •:

1.8 |  Please list the recent activities,  events and actions related to  the project that summarize the  project’s progress during the  monitoring period this report  refers to.:  | • Activities:   After  planting  wild  forage  peanuts  on  •  approximately 146 ha of the cocoa plantation in  2019,  this  project  continued  in  2021,  with  an  additional  88  ha  added.  The  advance  was  reduced to only 3 hectares in 2022 due to the  impact  of  a  tropical  storm  named  Bonnie  in  June and then the impact of a strong hurricane  in  October  called  Julia,  a  period  in  which  planting  is  planned  due  to  soil  humidity.  However,  in  2023,  386  hectares  have  been  planted,  and  it  is  intended  to  end  with  400  hectares. The planning for 2024 is to plant 400  more  hectares  of  forage  peanuts,  in  this  way,  we would be meeting the objective of covering  100% of the plantation. The benefits obtained  with  this  project  are:  Weed  control,  reduction  of  labor,  conservation  of  soil,  and  disease  control.  Furthermore,  according  to  studies  carried out, wild forage peanuts provide us with  1.44  kg  of  nitrogen  per  hectare  and  a  24%  increase in organic matter in the soil.  Compost application: 116 tons of compost  •  were applied as equal amounts at the non- planting area in the Finca (locally produced  out of cocoa pods, organic waste and Gandul  tree, which is grown for biomass purposes))  •  Soil analysis: Every year one big soil sampling  is carried out, this is being analyzed by a  laboratory in Costa Rica for macro and  micronutrients such as phosphorus nitrogen,  potassium, zinc, boron, calcium, magnesium,  manganese, and iron. We also carry out three  internal analyses where we monitor the PH  and the extractable acidity.    •  Water analysis. There were twelve analyses of  drinking water, which were carried out with  the farm’s internal laboratory in 2022.  Less permanent jobs: 36 compared to 2021.  •  Due to the high personnel fluctuation that  occurred in 2022. The main factor was  migration to various countries. This  phenomenon affected the entire country.  1290 hours of trainings for employees: Due to    the COVID-19 pandemic since 2020 there was  a general reduction in trainings, in order to  avoid agglomerations and external visitors as  well as due to travel limitations. Another  reason was the impact of Tropical Storm  Bonnie and Hurricane Julia in the second  semester general efforts were focused on  rehabilitation of the plantation and less on:

2. Zero hunger.summary: The evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Change in farmer income as a result of project, by gender and indigenous status'. Although the salary for permanent employees on El Cacao increased from 218.50 USD to 277.30 USD, and the salary for temporary staff increased from 132.60 USD to 159.40 USD, this information is not explicitly stated to be related to small-scale food producers. Additionally, the number of female permanent employees on El Cacao increased, but this does not directly measure income. The salary at the end of 2022 was 194 USD, which is higher than the national average, but this is not directly related to the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence is treated as qualitative (Score = 1).

The results stated that soil organic  matter increased at the Finca (from 5.54% in  2020 to 5.72% in 2021, although in 2022 a  reduction to 5.24% was reported. This could  have been due to the following factors:  Adverse climatic conditions (very high  precipitation, temperatures, and radiation);  potential sampling uncertainties. In addition,  due to the high amount of rain recorded, a  washout of bases occurs that increases the  acidity in the soil and decreases the pH, both  with negative results. 1.8 |  Please list the recent activities,  events and actions related to  the project that summarize the  project’s progress during the  monitoring period this report  refers to.: This may include milestones in  implementation, calibration of equipment,  carrying out of surveys or monitoring  activities, Host Country approvals, etc.  Refer to: Principles and Requirements  5.1.42. (a) | Activities:  Fertirrigation  project:  The  project  executed  in  •  2021  is  about  a  fertigation  system  in  cocoa  plantations  under  agroforestry  systems,  this  was  established  in  order  to  achieve  better  nutrient  assimilation  and  to  be  able  to  give  better soil management. The farm has a water  reservoir  which  is  working  in  the  supply  for  pumping  water  for  fertigation.  An  electrical  network was installed up to the site where the  fertigation  system  pumps  were  installed.  For  this  reason,  according  to  the  environmental  evaluation  system  (Decree  20-2017  that  establishes  the  bases  that  govern  the  environmental  evaluation  of  projects  and  activities in the country), it was classified as a  Project  of  moderate  potential  environmental  impact, the project was subject to a study for  the  processing  of  the  permit  application  according to the established procedures.   After  planting  wild  forage  peanuts  on  about  •  146  ha  of  the  cocoa  plantation  in  2019,  this  project was continued in 2021 with further 88  ha added. The goal is that by the end of 2023  the whole cocoa plantation will be planted with  wild forage peanuts. The benefits obtained from  this project are: Weed control, labor reduction,  soil  conservation,  and  Disease  control.  Furthermore,  according  to  the  studies  carried  out,  wild  forage  peanut  provides  us  1.44  nitrogen  kg/ha  and  an  increase  of  24%  of  organic matter in the soil.  Compost application: 123.5 tons of compost  •  were applied as equal amounts at the non- planting area in the Finca (locally produced  out of cocoa pods, organic waste and Gandul  tree, which is grown for biomasse purposes))  •  Soil analysis: Every year one big soil sampling  is carried out, this is being analyzed by a  laboratory in Costa Rica for macro and  micronutrients such as phosphorus nitrogen,  potassium, zinc, boron, calcium, magnesium,  manganese and iron. We also carry out three  internal analyzes where we monitor the PH  and the extractable acidity.  Evidence 05  o    •  Water analysis. There were twelve analyzes of  drinking water, which were carried out with  the farm’s internal laboratory. The water of  the river was last analyzed in 2020.   Evidence 06  o  New permanente jobs: 35 compared to 2020.  •  2000 hours of trainings for employees: Due to  •:

(a) | • Activities:   After  planting  wild  forage  peanuts  on  •  approximately 146 ha of the cocoa plantation in  2019,  this  project  continued  in  2021,  with  an  additional  88  ha  added.  The  advance  was  reduced to only 3 hectares in 2022 due to the  impact  of  a  tropical  storm  named  Bonnie  in  June and then the impact of a strong hurricane  in  October  called  Julia,  a  period  in  which  planting  is  planned  due  to  soil  humidity.  However,  in  2023,  386  hectares  have  been  planted,  and  it  is  intended  to  end  with  400  hectares. The planning for 2024 is to plant 400  more  hectares  of  forage  peanuts,  in  this  way,  we would be meeting the objective of covering  100% of the plantation. The benefits obtained  with  this  project  are:  Weed  control,  reduction  of  labor,  conservation  of  soil,  and  disease  control.  Furthermore,  according  to  studies  carried out, wild forage peanuts provide us with  1.44  kg  of  nitrogen  per  hectare  and  a  24%  increase in organic matter in the soil.  Compost application: 116 tons of compost  •  were applied as equal amounts at the non- planting area in the Finca (locally produced  out of cocoa pods, organic waste and Gandul  tree, which is grown for biomass purposes))  •  Soil analysis: Every year one big soil sampling  is carried out, this is being analyzed by a  laboratory in Costa Rica for macro and  micronutrients such as phosphorus nitrogen,  potassium, zinc, boron, calcium, magnesium,  manganese, and iron. We also carry out three  internal analyses where we monitor the PH  and the extractable acidity.    •  Water analysis. There were twelve analyses of  drinking water, which were carried out with  the farm’s internal laboratory in 2022. This could  have been due to the following factors:  Adverse climatic conditions (very high  precipitation, temperatures, and radiation);  potential sampling uncertainties. In addition,  due to the high amount of rain recorded, a  washout of bases occurs that increases the  acidity in the soil and decreases the pH, both  with negative results.   ID 4: Biodiversity: Indicator: Tree Crown  4.  cover. This analysis was not possible as we  assumed using the satellite images of Google  Earth where however the most recent updated  satellite image for the planting area is  available for the year 2015. There are no  further alternative options available on an  annual basis at alternative sites, as  globalforestwatch.org (GFW). At GFW there  are also specific biodiversity indicators  available, however, not on an annual basis.   However, as an alternative, a biodiversity  study with the University National Agraria was  implemented. In this study fauna and floral  species have been specified. The results  revealed the importance that farms like this  one can have, which are committed to the  protection and sustainable use of natural  resources. Several species have been found,  some of them are even in serious danger of  extinction. Add to this the large amount of  precious wood found, which in many cases is  the object of illegal trafficking, but this is not  the case on this farm. Undoubtedly, this  reveals that a good step has been taken  towards sustainability, using these production  systems. 1.8 |  Please list the recent activities,  events and actions related to  the project that summarize the  project’s progress during the  monitoring period this report  refers to.:  | Activities:  Fertirrigation  project:  The  project  executed  in  •  2021  is  about  a  fertigation  system  in  cocoa  plantations  under  agroforestry  systems,  this  was  established  in  order  to  achieve  better  nutrient  assimilation  and  to  be  able  to  give  better soil management. The farm has a water  reservoir  which  is  working  in  the  supply  for  pumping  water  for  fertigation.  An  electrical  network was installed up to the site where the  fertigation  system  pumps  were  installed.  For  this  reason,  according  to  the  environmental  evaluation  system  (Decree  20-2017  that  establishes  the  bases  that  govern  the  environmental  evaluation  of  projects  and  activities in the country), it was classified as a  Project  of  moderate  potential  environmental  impact, the project was subject to a study for  the  processing  of  the  permit  application  according to the established procedures.   After  planting  wild  forage  peanuts  on  about  •  146  ha  of  the  cocoa  plantation  in  2019,  this  project was continued in 2021 with further 88  ha added. The goal is that by the end of 2023  the whole cocoa plantation will be planted with  wild forage peanuts. The benefits obtained from  this project are: Weed control, labor reduction,  soil  conservation,  and  Disease  control.  Furthermore,  according  to  the  studies  carried  out,  wild  forage  peanut  provides  us  1.44  nitrogen  kg/ha  and  an  increase  of  24%  of  organic matter in the soil.  Compost application: 123.5 tons of compost  •  were applied as equal amounts at the non- planting area in the Finca (locally produced  out of cocoa pods, organic waste and Gandul  tree, which is grown for biomasse purposes))  •  Soil analysis: Every year one big soil sampling  is carried out, this is being analyzed by a  laboratory in Costa Rica for macro and  micronutrients such as phosphorus nitrogen,  potassium, zinc, boron, calcium, magnesium,  manganese and iron. We also carry out three  internal analyzes where we monitor the PH  and the extractable acidity.  Evidence 05  o    •  Water analysis. There were twelve analyzes of  drinking water, which were carried out with  the farm’s internal laboratory. The water of  the river was last analyzed in 2020. 3. Good health and well being.summary: The evidence provided does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Area under reduced/avoided open burning of biomass, crop residue'. Although the baseline area under open burning of biomass/crop residue before the implementation of sustainable land management practices is 1,755 ha (Document: GS4708+Validation+Report.pdf, Page: 65), the total land area where alternative technologies or measures are being implemented to avoid open biomass/crop residue burning is not explicitly stated. The project area is 2,574 ha, but it includes various land uses such as protected forest, non-eligible Tacotals of regenerated forest biomass, water bodies, and biomass production. Therefore, the evidence is qualitative only, and the score is 1.

Improvement through reforestation  ID 3: Soil condition: Soil organic  3.  carbon/matter content: A soil sampling  campaign has been implemented in different: years 2020, 2021 and 2022 (90 sampling  points). The results stated that soil organic  matter increased at the Finca (from 5.54% in  2020 to 5.72% in 2021, although in 2022 a  reduction to 5.24% was reported. This could  have been due to the following factors:  Adverse climatic conditions (very high  precipitation, temperatures, and radiation);  potential sampling uncertainties. In addition,  due to the high amount of rain recorded, a  washout of bases occurs that increases the  acidity in the soil and decreases the pH, both  with negative results.   ID 4: Biodiversity: Indicator: Tree Crown  4.  cover. This analysis was not possible as we  assumed using the satellite images of Google  Earth where however the most recent updated  satellite image for the planting area is  available for the year 2015. There are no  further alternative options available on an  annual basis at alternative sites, as  globalforestwatch.org (GFW). At GFW there  are also specific biodiversity indicators  available, however, not on an annual basis.   However, as an alternative, a biodiversity  study with the University National Agraria was  implemented. In this study fauna and floral  species have been specified. The results  revealed the importance that farms like this  one can have, which are committed to the  protection and sustainable use of natural  resources. Several species have been found,  some of them are even in serious danger of  extinction. Add to this the large amount of  precious wood found, which in many cases is  the object of illegal trafficking, but this is not  the case on this farm. Undoubtedly, this  reveals that a good step has been taken  towards sustainability, using these production  systems. Good health and well being.summary: The evidence from the project's annual report (Document: T-PerfCert_V20.1-Project-Annual-Report-Form_FV_22122023_signed.pdf, Page: 9) shows an improvement in air quality, water quality and quantity, and soil condition through reforestation. However, this evidence is qualitative and does not directly measure the number of farmers who have adopted alternative measures. The project owner's measures to prevent fires, such as training fire fighters and implementing necessary measures for fire prevention (Document: GS4708+Validation+Report.pdf, Page: 61), are also qualitative and do not provide explicit quantitative data. The project's location in a humid tropical climate zone with abundant water bodies and groundwater resources (Document: GS4708+Validation+Report.pdf, Page: 61) is contextual information and does not directly measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable, and the score is 0.

The results  revealed the importance that farms like this  one can have, which are committed to the  protection and sustainable use of natural  resources. Several species have been found,  some of them are even in serious danger of  extinction. Add to this the large amount of  precious wood found, which in many cases is  the object of illegal trafficking, but this is not  the case on this farm. Undoubtedly, this  reveals that a good step has been taken  towards sustainability, using these production  systems.  a. 4. Quality education.summary: The evidence provided does not directly measure the indicator variable, 'Number of employees provided skill development training'. Although the total number of person-hours for employees on El Cacao in 2015, 2016, and 2017 is provided as 890h, 1952h, and 1453h, respectively, and in 2022 was 1164h for 38 persons, this information is relevant to the quality of employment indicator, specifically the parameter of trainings and workshops. The total number of employees who received skill development training is not explicitly stated, and the proportion of employees who received skill development training compared to the total number of employees in 2022 is calculated as 22.3%, but this is based on an assumption of average training hours per person. Therefore, the evidence is treated conservatively, and the score is 1.

Therefore, the evidence is treated as qualitative (Score = 1).

Although it mentions a woman in charge of an agricultural sector with approximately 39 workers, this information does not provide the number of unique individual female managers employed by the organization. The total number of female employees in the organization is 37, but the number of female employees in managerial/leadership/ownership roles is not explicitly stated. Therefore, the evidence is treated as qualitative (Score = 1).

6. Clean water and sanitation.summary: The provided evidence does not directly measure the turbidity of river water. Although the project is located in a humid tropical climate zone with abundant water bodies and plentiful groundwater resources (Document: GS4708+Validation+Report.pdf, Page: 61), no specific information is provided on water quality trends or changes over time. The project implementation plan mentions that groundwater will be used for irrigation and will remain sufficient during the whole project period (Document: GS4708+Validation+Report.pdf, Page: 61), but this does not directly relate to the turbidity of river water. Therefore, the evidence fails to satisfy the measurement requirement of the indicator.

What is the state of water quality in wa.content: 1.14 | Please provide a summary of  the monitoring information  collected during the year.:  | ID 1: Air quality. Improvement through  1.  reforestation, no measurement  ID 2: Water quality and quantity:  2.  Improvement through reforestation  ID 3: Soil condition: Soil organic  3.  carbon/matter content: A soil sampling  campaign has been implemented in different: years 2020, 2021 and 2022 (90 sampling  points). The results stated that soil organic  matter increased at the Finca (from 5.54% in  2020 to 5.72% in 2021, although in 2022 a  reduction to 5.24% was reported. This could  have been due to the following factors:  Adverse climatic conditions (very high  precipitation, temperatures, and radiation);  potential sampling uncertainties. In addition,  due to the high amount of rain recorded, a  washout of bases occurs that increases the  acidity in the soil and decreases the pH, both  with negative results.   ID 4: Biodiversity: Indicator: Tree Crown  4.  cover. This analysis was not possible as we  assumed using the satellite images of Google  Earth where however the most recent updated  satellite image for the planting area is  available for the year 2015. There are no  further alternative options available on an  annual basis at alternative sites, as  globalforestwatch.org (GFW). At GFW there  are also specific biodiversity indicators  available, however, not on an annual basis.   However, as an alternative, a biodiversity  study with the University National Agraria was  implemented. In this study fauna and floral  species have been specified. The results  revealed the importance that farms like this  one can have, which are committed to the  protection and sustainable use of natural  resources. Several species have been found,  some of them are even in serious danger of  extinction. Add to this the large amount of  precious wood found, which in many cases is  the object of illegal trafficking, but this is not  the case on this farm. Undoubtedly, this  reveals that a good step has been taken  towards sustainability, using these production  systems.    ID 5: Quality of employment: The number of  5.  person-hours training was 1164h (for 38  persons) in 2022 (English language courses,  training on quality control of cocoa beans,  national law etc.)  ID 6: Livelihood of the poor: + ID 8:  6.

Clean water and sanitation.summary: The evidence from GS4708+Validation+Report.pdf, Page 61, mentions that the project area has abundant water bodies and plentiful groundwater resources, but does not provide explicit quantitative data to measure the water retention capacity. Although the document mentions that the planted trees will grow without damage and the groundwater will remain sufficient, this is a qualitative statement and does not directly measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the measurement requirement of the indicator.

Affordable and clean energy.summary: The validation report (GS4708+Validation+Report.pdf, Page: 57) explicitly states that the indicator score for 'Access to affordable and clean energy services' is defined as 0. This satisfies the indicator's measurement requirement as it directly measures the indicator variable. However, the report also mentions that the project has implemented measures to reduce synthetic fertilizers and introduce compost (GS4708+Validation+Report.pdf, Page: 47), which is a qualitative statement and does not directly measure the indicator variable. The project has paused planting new areas due to El Niño prognostics for 2024 (T-PerfCert_V20.1-Project-Annual-Report-Form_FV_22122023_signed.pdf, Page: 5), which may indicate a lack of investment in clean energy infrastructure, but this is not a direct measurement of the indicator variable.

8. Decent work and economic growth.summary: The evidence provided in the Intermediate Answers and Context directly measures the indicator variable, 'Total number of jobs'. The total number of jobs generated as a result of the project is 623, calculated by adding the number of permanent and temporary jobs in 2022 and 2021, and the number of permanent jobs expected to grow up to. This evidence is found in the documents: T-PerfCert_V20.1-Project-Annual-Report-Form_FV_22122023_signed.pdf (Page: 10), T-PerfCert_V2.0-Project-Annual-Report-Form_DV3_EN_22122022_sign.pdf (Page: 9), and GS4708+Validation+Report.pdf (Page: 36). The evidence satisfies the indicator's measurement requirement as it provides explicit quantitative data that directly measures the indicator variable.

Therefore, the evidence is treated as qualitative, and the score is 1.

The average salary for permanent employees on El Cacao at the end of 2022 was 194 USD, and the average number of permanent jobs in 2022 was 205, but these numbers do not directly measure the income generation from ecotourism activities. The context mentions the project's financial means and secured funds, but it does not provide information on income generation from ecotourism activities. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

Improvement through reforestation  ID 3: Soil condition: Soil organic  3.  carbon/matter content: A soil sampling  campaign has been implemented in different: years 2020, 2021 and 2022 (90 sampling  points). The results stated that soil organic  matter increased at the Finca (from 5.54% in  2020 to 5.72% in 2021, although in 2022 a  reduction to 5.24% was reported. This could  have been due to the following factors:  Adverse climatic conditions (very high  precipitation, temperatures, and radiation);  potential sampling uncertainties. In addition,  due to the high amount of rain recorded, a  washout of bases occurs that increases the  acidity in the soil and decreases the pH, both  with negative results.   ID 4: Biodiversity: Indicator: Tree Crown  4.  cover. This analysis was not possible as we  assumed using the satellite images of Google  Earth where however the most recent updated  satellite image for the planting area is  available for the year 2015. There are no  further alternative options available on an  annual basis at alternative sites, as  globalforestwatch.org (GFW). At GFW there  are also specific biodiversity indicators  available, however, not on an annual basis.   However, as an alternative, a biodiversity  study with the University National Agraria was  implemented. In this study fauna and floral  species have been specified. The results  revealed the importance that farms like this  one can have, which are committed to the  protection and sustainable use of natural  resources. Several species have been found,  some of them are even in serious danger of  extinction. Add to this the large amount of  precious wood found, which in many cases is  the object of illegal trafficking, but this is not  the case on this farm. Undoubtedly, this  reveals that a good step has been taken  towards sustainability, using these production  systems. However, no quantitative values or specific targets are mentioned to assess the effectiveness of these measures. The project has complied with the Gold Standard Principles, but this does not directly measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence is qualitative and does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

The project's reforestation efforts and soil organic matter increase are relevant but qualitative (Score = 1). The death rate of replanted trees and validated biomass growth downsizing factor are quantitative, but do not measure the indicator variable directly (Score = 1). The reduction of non-renewable wood fuel consumption is not explicitly mentioned in the provided context. The evidence does not provide explicit quantitative data that directly measures or clearly supports the indicator.

Date: 24.06.2019  6B  4.1: With regard to the Spatial assessment forest/non-forest  of  the  project  area  to  classify  eligible  areas  for  planting  in  tacotales  and  cocoa  plantation  the  following observations were made:    3.  Huge cloudy areas on the satellite image have been analyzed by the automatic process as eligible  areas (bushland and open areas. It is with no doubt visible that these areas are clouds.     4.  The pixel size of the satellite imagery is 0,5m x 0,5m = 0,25 m² (see metadata of the image). In the  analysis 4m x 4m is written, which is not correct. Further each stand-alone single pixel has been  classified as eligible area. This makes no sense in terms auf afforesting these small forest gaps of  0,25m². Also sizes of 6x6 Pixel would only represent an area of 9m² which is not eligible for  planting a single tree. Only areas can be eligible that have a minimum size of 1 ha can be eligible  when placed surrounded by forest (otherwise they have to be classified as forest as per DNA  classification).    It is concluded that the estimated area for eligible planting area (42 ha) in tacotales is overestimated  when taking the satellite image from 2011 into account.

Clarification  is  requested  what  is  the  actual  barrier  for  this  alternative  (and  not  in  comparison  with  other  alternatives)  and  how  this  barrier is preventing the alternative of being implemented including documented evidence.  3.  Following issues have been identified on the common practice analysis   a.  Further specification w.r.t. extent of implementation/use of the programmes is missing.  b.  Clarification  and  specification  is  requested  w.r.t.  number  of  forestry  projects  ongoing  in  Nicaragua.

1.  Not  ok.  As  per  4.1_-_template_-_ar_cdm_additionality_tool_DV4.docx  under  step  1a  no  further alternatives have been mentioned and under step 1b the stated alternatives are given  but no description and related justification/assessment is provided. The box is either empty or  the  following  is  stated  “Cocoa  cultivation  under  an  intensive  management  would  have  been  possible too. However,”. Pls clarify.  2.  The document has been revised accordingly. Each alternative has been assessed separately.  The following barriers have been identified by PP::  | : 2 | : Forestation  without  being  registered  as  GS project | : First-of-its-kind  and  other  barrier | : As  per  check  of  project  database  of  GS,  CDM  and  VCS  the  following forest projects in the host country could be identified:  CDM:  3970: Southern Nicaragua CDM Reforestation Project  GS:  Sustainable Forest Plantations Leon (ID: 103000000010205)  VCS:  1085: ECOPLANET BAMBOO CENTRAL AMERICA  The  CDM  project  and  the  VCS  project  are  reforestation  projects  however  of  different  type.  The  CDM  project  is  a  reforestation  project to generate renewable wood supplies and the VCS project  is a bamboo plantation. Therefore both projects are not related to  plantation of trees for harvesting   The  GS  project  is  also  considering  cocoa  besides  Honey  and  sustainable  agriculture  but  is  different  from  this  project  as  local  farmers  (currently  120  on  467  ha,  http://www.proclimate.org/portfolio-item/reforestation/  )  are  trained  on  sustainable  farming  whereas  this  project  is  operated  by  one  owner  and  is  a  large  scale  sustainable  cocoa  plantation  project  (2574  ha  total  area  and  1514  ha  planted  area).  The  size  of  sustainable farming of the other GS project is 467ha/120 = 3.89ha  per  farmer  and  therefore  different  farming  management  is  required.  A  small  area  is  much  easier  to  be  managed  and  sustainably operated than large scale area.  Further as evidence by3 there is   - Lack of technical know how with regard to timber plantation  establishment and maintenance  - Qualified personnel and examples of success are scarce  - Very little experience exists in Nicaragua in producing tropical  quality wood via reforestation  Further plantations are more dependent on long-term stability than  cattle breeding. For a plantation, at least 18 years without  disturbance are indispensible and therefore the risk on investment  is much higher than for a project type of supporting local farmers.  The high amount of investment is confirmed by checking the  company licence applications and business licence.  Finally The historical plantation rate in Nicaragua is estimated at  about 3500-4000 ha per year (including energy wood). The area  suitable for reforestation is estimated at 2.5 Million ha. Most of the  reforestations established in the past have been done with the  assistance of international development organizations.  Privately financed reforestations have been carried out  occasionally by a few individual parties. The rate of success for  past reforestations is reported to be very low and many plantations  were poorly maintained, abandoned or re-converted to agricultural  land4, 5,6. Based on that the validation team confirms the project is  first of its kind w.r.t. sustainable large scale reforestation project in  the host country applying cocoa plantation as well as on other  barriers. | :

This analysis was not possible as we  assumed using the satellite images of Google  Earth where however the most recent updated  satellite image for the planting area is  available for the year 2015. There are no  further alternative options available on an  annual basis at alternative sites, as  globalforestwatch.org (GFW). At GFW there  are also specific biodiversity indicators  available, however, not on an annual basis.   However, as an alternative, a biodiversity  study with the University National Agraria was  implemented. In this study fauna and floral  species have been specified. The results  revealed the importance that farms like this  one can have, which are committed to the  protection and sustainable use of natural  resources. Several species have been found,  some of them are even in serious danger of  extinction. Add to this the large amount of  precious wood found, which in many cases is  the object of illegal trafficking, but this is not  the case on this farm. Undoubtedly, this  reveals that a good step has been taken  towards sustainability, using these production  systems. 15. Life on land.indicator: Forest areas managed sustainably for forest products including sustainable produced fuelwood

15. Life on land.summary: The provided evidence does not explicitly state the total above and below ground biomass stock in forests within the specific geographic scope. Although the biomass expansion factor (BF) is mentioned as 0.29, and the Root-to-Shoot ratio calculated as a ratio of estimated quantity of above and below ground biomass is 0.29, these values do not directly measure the indicator variable. The evidence is qualitative only, and no explicit quantitative data is provided to directly measure or clearly support the indicator. Therefore, the evidence fails to satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

It is concluded that the estimated area for eligible planting area (42 ha) in tacotales is overestimated  when taking the satellite image from 2011 into account.

Date: 24.06.2019  6B  4.1: After the first submission the land use classification of the Tacotal areas was evaluated as not  adequate by the VVB, as several areas covered by clouds or cloud shades were shown as eligible  planting area there. 1.8 |  Please list the recent activities,  events and actions related to  the project that summarize the  project’s progress during the  monitoring period this report  refers to.:  | • Activities:   After  planting  wild  forage  peanuts  on  •  approximately 146 ha of the cocoa plantation in  2019,  this  project  continued  in  2021,  with  an  additional  88  ha  added.  The  advance  was  reduced to only 3 hectares in 2022 due to the  impact  of  a  tropical  storm  named  Bonnie  in  June and then the impact of a strong hurricane  in  October  called  Julia,  a  period  in  which  planting  is  planned  due  to  soil  humidity.  However,  in  2023,  386  hectares  have  been  planted,  and  it  is  intended  to  end  with  400  hectares. The planning for 2024 is to plant 400  more  hectares  of  forage  peanuts,  in  this  way,  we would be meeting the objective of covering  100% of the plantation. The benefits obtained  with  this  project  are:  Weed  control,  reduction  of  labor,  conservation  of  soil,  and  disease  control.  Furthermore,  according  to  studies  carried out, wild forage peanuts provide us with  1.44  kg  of  nitrogen  per  hectare  and  a  24%  increase in organic matter in the soil.  Compost application: 116 tons of compost  •  were applied as equal amounts at the non- planting area in the Finca (locally produced  out of cocoa pods, organic waste and Gandul  tree, which is grown for biomass purposes))  •  Soil analysis: Every year one big soil sampling  is carried out, this is being analyzed by a  laboratory in Costa Rica for macro and  micronutrients such as phosphorus nitrogen,  potassium, zinc, boron, calcium, magnesium,  manganese, and iron. We also carry out three  internal analyses where we monitor the PH  and the extractable acidity.    •  Water analysis. There were twelve analyses of  drinking water, which were carried out with  the farm’s internal laboratory in 2022.  Less permanent jobs: 36 compared to 2021.  •  Due to the high personnel fluctuation that  occurred in 2022. The main factor was  migration to various countries. This  phenomenon affected the entire country.  1290 hours of trainings for employees: Due to    the COVID-19 pandemic since 2020 there was  a general reduction in trainings, in order to  avoid agglomerations and external visitors as  well as due to travel limitations. Another  reason was the impact of Tropical Storm  Bonnie and Hurricane Julia in the second  semester general efforts were focused on  rehabilitation of the plantation and less on:

15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided in the Intermediate Answers and Context does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Total Area of Trees Planted: Native Species'. The total land area where native species were planted is 1,755 ha, but this is not explicitly stated as a percentage of the total planted trees. The context mentions that 117 trees and 936 cocoa plants per ha were planted, but it does not provide information on the number of native trees planted. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

1.  Not  ok.  As  per  4.1_-_template_-_ar_cdm_additionality_tool_DV4.docx  under  step  1a  no  further alternatives have been mentioned and under step 1b the stated alternatives are given  but no description and related justification/assessment is provided. The box is either empty or  the  following  is  stated  “Cocoa  cultivation  under  an  intensive  management  would  have  been  possible too. However,”. Pls clarify.  2.  The document has been revised accordingly. Each alternative has been assessed separately.  The following barriers have been identified by PP::  | : 2 | : Forestation  without  being  registered  as  GS project | : First-of-its-kind  and  other  barrier | : As  per  check  of  project  database  of  GS,  CDM  and  VCS  the  following forest projects in the host country could be identified:  CDM:  3970: Southern Nicaragua CDM Reforestation Project  GS:  Sustainable Forest Plantations Leon (ID: 103000000010205)  VCS:  1085: ECOPLANET BAMBOO CENTRAL AMERICA  The  CDM  project  and  the  VCS  project  are  reforestation  projects  however  of  different  type.  The  CDM  project  is  a  reforestation  project to generate renewable wood supplies and the VCS project  is a bamboo plantation. Therefore both projects are not related to  plantation of trees for harvesting   The  GS  project  is  also  considering  cocoa  besides  Honey  and  sustainable  agriculture  but  is  different  from  this  project  as  local  farmers  (currently  120  on  467  ha,  http://www.proclimate.org/portfolio-item/reforestation/  )  are  trained  on  sustainable  farming  whereas  this  project  is  operated  by  one  owner  and  is  a  large  scale  sustainable  cocoa  plantation  project  (2574  ha  total  area  and  1514  ha  planted  area).  The  size  of  sustainable farming of the other GS project is 467ha/120 = 3.89ha  per  farmer  and  therefore  different  farming  management  is  required.  A  small  area  is  much  easier  to  be  managed  and  sustainably operated than large scale area.  Further as evidence by3 there is   - Lack of technical know how with regard to timber plantation  establishment and maintenance  - Qualified personnel and examples of success are scarce  - Very little experience exists in Nicaragua in producing tropical  quality wood via reforestation  Further plantations are more dependent on long-term stability than  cattle breeding. For a plantation, at least 18 years without  disturbance are indispensible and therefore the risk on investment  is much higher than for a project type of supporting local farmers.  The high amount of investment is confirmed by checking the  company licence applications and business licence.  Finally The historical plantation rate in Nicaragua is estimated at  about 3500-4000 ha per year (including energy wood). The area  suitable for reforestation is estimated at 2.5 Million ha. Most of the  reforestations established in the past have been done with the  assistance of international development organizations.  Privately financed reforestations have been carried out  occasionally by a few individual parties. The rate of success for  past reforestations is reported to be very low and many plantations  were poorly maintained, abandoned or re-converted to agricultural  land4, 5,6. Based on that the validation team confirms the project is  first of its kind w.r.t. sustainable large scale reforestation project in  the host country applying cocoa plantation as well as on other  barriers. | :

Life on land.summary: The evidence from the GS4708+Validation+Report.pdf, Page: 46, mentions that an area with almost 36% of the project area has been managed to protect or enhance the biological diversity of native ecosystems according to the HCV approach. However, this information does not directly measure the number of protected threatened species in the project area. The project area includes 300 ha of protected forest, but no specific information is provided on the protection of threatened species. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

15. Life on land.target: 15.5 Protect and prevent extinction of threatened species
15. Life on land.indicator: Number of protected threatened species in the project area & conservation status of species

15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Trends in species diversity' as it lacks explicit quantitative data on the number of tree species present in the forest ecosystem. Although the project owner replanted 12,395 forest trees in 2023, including cocoa trees and Gmelina arborea, the total number of tree species is not specified. The percentage of the total tree population consisting of native species is estimated as 36% based on the project area managed to protect or enhance native ecosystems, but this is not a direct measure of species diversity. Therefore, the evidence is treated conservatively, and the score is 1.

Date: 24.06.2019  6B  4.1: With regard to the Spatial assessment forest/non-forest  of  the  project  area  to  classify  eligible  areas  for  planting  in  tacotales  and  cocoa  plantation  the  following observations were made:    3.  Huge cloudy areas on the satellite image have been analyzed by the automatic process as eligible  areas (bushland and open areas. It is with no doubt visible that these areas are clouds.     4.  The pixel size of the satellite imagery is 0,5m x 0,5m = 0,25 m² (see metadata of the image). In the  analysis 4m x 4m is written, which is not correct. Further each stand-alone single pixel has been  classified as eligible area. This makes no sense in terms auf afforesting these small forest gaps of  0,25m². Also sizes of 6x6 Pixel would only represent an area of 9m² which is not eligible for  planting a single tree. Only areas can be eligible that have a minimum size of 1 ha can be eligible  when placed surrounded by forest (otherwise they have to be classified as forest as per DNA  classification).    It is concluded that the estimated area for eligible planting area (42 ha) in tacotales is overestimated  when taking the satellite image from 2011 into account.

This causes damages to the affected trees on  about 92 ha, but very little trees are lost. Out of the  92 ha there were 33 ha affected within the planting  area. The range of plant mortality in this area was  due to that the heavy rainfalls worsened dramatically  the previous affectation of diseases such as stem  cancer, pests such as Xyleborus and Phytoptora in  this area. This caused the death of trees after the  heavy rainfalls.  Additionally, in October 2022, there was a large  hurricane above Nicaragua, which also impacted  many farmers and their land. It was also damaging  parts of the project area, including some trees. The  project owner finished an investigation of the forest  trees (see file “Mortality Forestales Huracan  2022.xlsx” and “Mortality Cacao y leguminosas  Hurrican.xlsx”). It shows that 9,289 forest trees  (36% of the forest trees), 22,775 cocoa trees and  12,277 legume trees were heavily damaged  (“mortality”) and could not survive. It is not yet clear  how the situation was on the sampling plots. The last  tree sampling campaign at the sampling plots was in  2021 (regular). The next sampling campaign in 2026  could reveal the damage to the sampling plots.  However, at the time of third-party verification, the  sampling plots could be visited focusing on damaged  trees within the sampling plots.  The project owner initiated the replanting activity,  e.g. replant damaged forest trees to the previous  density of 30 forest trees per hectare (more than  9,289 trees in total) and also replanting cocoa trees. Life on land.summary: The evidence from Document: GS4708+Validation+Report.pdf, Page: 50, states that no invasive species were discovered other than natural and indigenous grasses and other cover crops. However, the project aims to enhance habitat connectivity for flora and fauna through the planting areas and buffer zones, and native tree species will be planted as shade trees between cocoa trees. This information does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Trends in species distribution' as it does not provide explicit quantitative data on the distribution and traits of native tree species. Therefore, the evidence satisfies the indicator's measurement requirement only partially, but not to the extent of assigning a score of 2.

However, only 300 ha of this area is classified as protected forest, which is approximately 11.6% of the total land area. The evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable, which refers to total land area sustainably managed under project. Therefore, the score is 1, as the evidence is relevant but qualitative only.

What are the reported outcomes of these.content: 1.11 | Have there been any incidents  or events that may impact the  Outcomes/Impacts delivered to  date (in terms of loss) or the  ongoing Performance of the  Project.  Refer to Principles and Requirements  5.1.4.2.(d): 1.12 | Have any legal  contests/disputes arisen related  to the project?  Refer to Principles and Requirements  5.1.4.2.(e) | T ☒ Yes  ☐ No  If yes, please elaborate:  In 2022 there were two floodings of the river due to  heavy rains caused by the passage of tropical storm  Bonnie. This causes damages to the affected trees on  about 92 ha, but very little trees are lost. Out of the  92 ha there were 33 ha affected within the planting  area. The range of plant mortality in this area was  due to that the heavy rainfalls worsened dramatically  the previous affectation of diseases such as stem  cancer, pests such as Xyleborus and Phytoptora in  this area. This caused the death of trees after the  heavy rainfalls.  Additionally, in October 2022, there was a large  hurricane above Nicaragua, which also impacted  many farmers and their land. It was also damaging  parts of the project area, including some trees. The  project owner finished an investigation of the forest  trees (see file “Mortality Forestales Huracan  2022.xlsx” and “Mortality Cacao y leguminosas  Hurrican.xlsx”). It shows that 9,289 forest trees  (36% of the forest trees), 22,775 cocoa trees and  12,277 legume trees were heavily damaged  (“mortality”) and could not survive. It is not yet clear  how the situation was on the sampling plots. The last  tree sampling campaign at the sampling plots was in  2021 (regular). The next sampling campaign in 2026  could reveal the damage to the sampling plots.  However, at the time of third-party verification, the  sampling plots could be visited focusing on damaged  trees within the sampling plots.  The project owner initiated the replanting activity,  e.g. replant damaged forest trees to the previous  density of 30 forest trees per hectare (more than  9,289 trees in total) and also replanting cocoa trees.  In 2023, 12,395 forest trees are being replanted.  he mitigation measures for the impact of the  COVID-19 pandemic on the company remain in force,  the installation of disinfection devices, increased  means of transportation to maintain a greater  distance between workers, and the purchase of  vitamin C to strengthen the immune system of all our  staff.: ☐ Yes  ☒ No  If yes, please elaborate:

Improvement through reforestation  ID 3: Soil condition: Soil organic  3.  carbon/matter content: A soil sampling  campaign has been implemented in different: years 2020, 2021 and 2022 (90 sampling  points). The results stated that soil organic  matter increased at the Finca (from 5.54% in  2020 to 5.72% in 2021, although in 2022 a  reduction to 5.24% was reported. This could  have been due to the following factors:  Adverse climatic conditions (very high  precipitation, temperatures, and radiation);  potential sampling uncertainties. In addition,  due to the high amount of rain recorded, a  washout of bases occurs that increases the  acidity in the soil and decreases the pH, both  with negative results.   ID 4: Biodiversity: Indicator: Tree Crown  4.  cover. This analysis was not possible as we  assumed using the satellite images of Google  Earth where however the most recent updated  satellite image for the planting area is  available for the year 2015. There are no  further alternative options available on an  annual basis at alternative sites, as  globalforestwatch.org (GFW). At GFW there  are also specific biodiversity indicators  available, however, not on an annual basis.   However, as an alternative, a biodiversity  study with the University National Agraria was  implemented. In this study fauna and floral  species have been specified. The results  revealed the importance that farms like this  one can have, which are committed to the  protection and sustainable use of natural  resources. Several species have been found,  some of them are even in serious danger of  extinction. Add to this the large amount of  precious wood found, which in many cases is  the object of illegal trafficking, but this is not  the case on this farm. Undoubtedly, this  reveals that a good step has been taken  towards sustainability, using these production  systems. 1.8 |  Please list the recent activities,  events and actions related to  the project that summarize the  project’s progress during the  monitoring period this report  refers to.: This may include milestones in  implementation, calibration of equipment,  carrying out of surveys or monitoring  activities, Host Country approvals, etc.  Refer to: Principles and Requirements  5.1.42. (a) | Activities:  Fertirrigation  project:  The  project  executed  in  •  2021  is  about  a  fertigation  system  in  cocoa  plantations  under  agroforestry  systems,  this  was  established  in  order  to  achieve  better  nutrient  assimilation  and  to  be  able  to  give  better soil management. The farm has a water  reservoir  which  is  working  in  the  supply  for  pumping  water  for  fertigation.  An  electrical  network was installed up to the site where the  fertigation  system  pumps  were  installed.  For  this  reason,  according  to  the  environmental  evaluation  system  (Decree  20-2017  that  establishes  the  bases  that  govern  the  environmental  evaluation  of  projects  and  activities in the country), it was classified as a  Project  of  moderate  potential  environmental  impact, the project was subject to a study for  the  processing  of  the  permit  application  according to the established procedures.   After  planting  wild  forage  peanuts  on  about  •  146  ha  of  the  cocoa  plantation  in  2019,  this  project was continued in 2021 with further 88  ha added. The goal is that by the end of 2023  the whole cocoa plantation will be planted with  wild forage peanuts. The benefits obtained from  this project are: Weed control, labor reduction,  soil  conservation,  and  Disease  control.  Furthermore,  according  to  the  studies  carried  out,  wild  forage  peanut  provides  us  1.44  nitrogen  kg/ha  and  an  increase  of  24%  of  organic matter in the soil.  Compost application: 123.5 tons of compost  •  were applied as equal amounts at the non- planting area in the Finca (locally produced  out of cocoa pods, organic waste and Gandul  tree, which is grown for biomasse purposes))  •  Soil analysis: Every year one big soil sampling  is carried out, this is being analyzed by a  laboratory in Costa Rica for macro and  micronutrients such as phosphorus nitrogen,  potassium, zinc, boron, calcium, magnesium,  manganese and iron. We also carry out three  internal analyzes where we monitor the PH  and the extractable acidity.  Evidence 05  o    •  Water analysis. There were twelve analyzes of  drinking water, which were carried out with  the farm’s internal laboratory. The water of  the river was last analyzed in 2020.   Evidence 06  o  New permanente jobs: 35 compared to 2020.  •  2000 hours of trainings for employees: Due to  •:

As  long  as  the  production  of  cocoa  on  the  project  area  is  attractive the PP will have no incentive to change the land  use.  Moreover  the  project  area  is  divided  in  planting  and  protection  area  with  natural  and  second  forests.
